ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set; mapped to broader pork dish concept FOODON_00002473 as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork based composite dish with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set; A0FJP (pork-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean composite dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice, one breaded and pan-fried pork chop, and accompanying sauce with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set (cr_id G00010388). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The pork chop and sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 113.4g carbohydrate in 450g serving) / 100 ≈ 40.8. This is a high glycemic load meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g; full set meal delivers approximately 1040 mg sodium per serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced sauce (tomato sauce, Worcestershire sauce, and soy sauce are the primary sodium contributors) and limit the portion of breaded pork chop, as a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1040 mg sodium — nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100g; full serving delivers approximately 113 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~41
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice or reduce the rice portion by half to lower the glycemic load, avoid the breaded coating on the pork chop where possible, and monitor total carbohydrate intake carefully as a standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 113 g of carbohydrate with a high glycemic load of ~41.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Breaded and pan-fried pork chop contributes saturated fat and potential trans fat from frying; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g by HPB but pork chop inherently contains cholesterol — value warrants caution
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit the frequency of consumption of this dish due to the breaded and pan-fried pork chop which contributes saturated fat and frying-derived trans fat; requesting a grilled or baked pork chop preparation and reducing the sauce portion will lower the saturated fat and sodium burden per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from pork, AGE generation from breaded pan-frying, and nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, potatoes)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the pro-inflammatory combination of pork-derived arachidonic acid, advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated during breaded pan-frying, and nightshade vegetables (tomatoes and potatoes) which may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set (海南猪排饭套餐) is a quintessential Singaporean hawker and coffee shop dish with Hainanese immigrant origins. The set consists of steamed white jasmine rice served alongside a breaded and pan-fried pork chop, accompanied by a tangy tomato-based sauce made with Worcestershire sauce, onions, green peas, and sometimes potato wedges. The pork chop is typically tenderised, coated in egg and breadcrumbs, and shallow-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining juicy inside. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly tangy, and mildly sweet from the tomato sauce, with a satisfying contrast between the crispy breaded coating and the tender pork. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate with 3.7g dietary fibre. A standard 450g set meal delivers approximately 738 kcal, 29g protein, 19g fat, and 113g carbohydrate. The dish is high in glycemic load due to the white rice base (GI ~72) and is a moderate sodium source at 231mg/100g (~1040mg per serving). It is not suitable for Type 2 Diabetes patients without modification (brown rice substitution recommended), is of moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, and should be avoided by rheumatoid arthritis patients due to nightshade ingredients and AGE-generating preparation. The dish is a cultural staple at Singaporean kopitiams and hawker centres, commonly consumed at lunch and dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010388) with a provenance confidence of 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact — the HPB entry is specifically for Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (G00010388) as of April 2026. No supplementary approved source was available to fill these gaps without risking unsupported estimation. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this record. This is noted as potentially anomalous given that pork chop inherently contains cholesterol; the HPB value is preserved as reported but flagged for review. The value 0.0 is retained as confirmed per the HPB source. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct composite GI measurement for this dish was found in approved sources. 4. Serving size of 450g is an estimate based on typical Singaporean hawker set meal portions; no HPB-specified serving weight was available for this record. 5. FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as analogous estimate; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Hainanese Pork Chop Rice Set. 6. is_halal set to false as pork is a primary ingredient.
